content.text: A quarterly roadmap answers 'what are we building next?'; a multi-quarter roadmap answers 'where is the programme going over the next year, and in what order?'. Without the longer view, teams make locally rational decisions that create global problems — choosing a feature that delivers this quarter but blocks a more important one next quarter, or building infrastructure that duplicates planned platform work two quarters out.
A roadmap is not a commitment list for future quarters — it is a thinking tool. The further out a quarter is, the lower the confidence. Treat Q3–Q4 as direction, not contract.

The roadmap organises work across two dimensions: time (quarters) and themes (strategic problem areas). Themes cut vertically across quarters — a theme like 'Internal Process Automation' may have work items in every quarter of the year. This structure makes sequencing dependencies visible: the Q2 'Scale' work on document types depends on the Q1 'Foundation' MVP being shipped.
Themes are stable for at least two quarters. They represent the
the programme is investing in, not the specific solutions. Good themes are outcome-oriented; bad themes are technology-oriented.
Themes should map directly to programme-level OKR objectives. If you cannot trace a theme to an OKR, it is either missing from the OKR cascade or it is work that should not be in the programme.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class Confidence(Enum):
    HIGH   = "high"    # Q1: committed, scoped, staffed
    MEDIUM = "medium"  # Q2: planned, estimated, unconfirmed staffing
    LOW    = "low"     # Q3–Q4: directional intent; no detailed scope

class RoadmapItemStatus(Enum):
    NOT_STARTED = "not_started"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    COMPLETED   = "completed"
    DEFERRED    = "deferred"
    CANCELLED   = "cancelled"

@dataclass
class RoadmapTheme:
    theme_id: str
    name: str                        # e.g. "Internal Process Automation"
    description: str
    owning_okr_objective_id: str     # must map to a programme OKR
    colour_hex: str = "#6366f1"      # for display

@dataclass
class RoadmapItem:
    item_id: str
    theme_id: str
    title: str
    quarter: str                     # "2026-Q1", "2026-Q2", etc.
    estimated_person_weeks: float
    skill_mix: dict[str, float]      # {"ml_engineer": 3.0, "data_engineer": 2.0}
    confidence: Confidence
    status: RoadmapItemStatus = RoadmapItemStatus.NOT_STARTED
    dependencies: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # other item_ids
    parent_request_id: Optional[str] = None   # links to intake portal request
    delivery_milestone: Optional[str] = None  # the user-visible outcome on completion

@dataclass
class MultiQuarterRoadmap:
    programme: str
    planning_year: int
    themes: list[RoadmapTheme]
    items: list[RoadmapItem]

    def items_for_quarter(self, quarter: str) -> list[RoadmapItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.quarter == quarter]

    def items_for_theme(self, theme_id: str) -> list[RoadmapItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.theme_id == theme_id]

    def total_pw_by_quarter(self) -> dict[str, float]:
        result: dict[str, float] = {}
        for item in self.items:
            result[item.quarter] = result.get(item.quarter, 0) + item.estimated_person_weeks
        return result

The most valuable function of a multi-quarter roadmap is explicit sequencing: identifying which items must precede others and ensuring the ordering is reflected in the quarterly plan. Three types of sequencing matter:
Item B cannot start until Item A is complete. Common in GenAI programmes:
Data pipeline must be production-stable before model training can begin
LLM gateway must have rate-card billing before cross-charging teams can be enabled
Evaluation framework must be in place before fine-tuning can be assessed objectively
Item B will be significantly faster or cheaper if Item A is done first, but can proceed without it at higher cost or risk. These should still be surfaced — they inform sequencing decisions.

Item B's scope or approach depends on what is learned from Item A. Common for exploratory GenAI work: a proof-of-concept in Q1 informs whether the Q2 production build targets 10,000 or 100,000 daily users.
Confidence Gradient — How Firm is the Plan?
The detail and commitment level of roadmap items should decrease as distance from the present increases. Planning too precisely for Q4 in January is waste — the context will have changed by then.
Fully scoped, staffed, sprint-planned, dependencies confirmed
Sized (±30 %), skill mix known, dependencies identified, not fully staffed
Theme + rough size (T-shirt: S/M/L/XL), sequencing intent only
Theme only; items are placeholders subject to full replacement
The roadmap is a living document, not a locked plan. Three review types keep it accurate:

: the COE and programme lead review all MEDIUM-confidence items for the next quarter. Items that have been scoped and staffed are promoted to HIGH. Items with unresolved blockers are flagged for executive attention.

: at the end of each quarter, LOW-confidence items for Q+2 are converted to MEDIUM or dropped. New items from the scored backlog are inserted. Capacity is checked against the full committed item list.
: themes are reassessed against updated company OKRs. New themes may be introduced; themes that have delivered their purpose are retired.

A multi-quarter roadmap needs two forms: a visual timeline for stakeholders who consume it passively, and a structured data export for systems that consume it programmatically (capacity planning tools, portfolio trackers, OKR systems).

Once a roadmap is published, stakeholders will request changes. An uncontrolled change process erodes the plan's credibility — if anything can be inserted at any time, the roadmap becomes noise. A simple change request process preserves the value of the published plan while remaining genuinely responsive.
2 business days; must defer equal PW to make room
At monthly review; no mid-cycle changes
At monthly review; capacity check required
Immediate; notify affected teams and dependent items

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

ChangeType = Literal[
    "insert_high",
    "promote_medium_to_high",
    "add_medium",
    "update_low",
    "cancel_committed",
]

@dataclass
class RoadmapChangeRequest:
    change_id: str
    requested_by: str
    requested_date: date
    change_type: ChangeType
    affected_item_id: str
    justification: str               # why this change is needed now
    displaced_item_id: str | None    # which item is deferred to make room (for inserts)
    approved_by: str | None = None
    approved_date: date | None = None
    status: str = "pending"          # "pending" | "approved" | "rejected"

def approve_change(
    change: RoadmapChangeRequest,
    approver: str,
    approval_date: date,
    roadmap: MultiQuarterRoadmap,
) -> None:
    """Apply the change to the roadmap after approval."""
    change.approved_by = approver
    change.approved_date = approval_date
    change.status = "approved"
    item_by_id = {i.item_id: i for i in roadmap.items}

    if change.change_type == "cancel_committed":
        item = item_by_id.get(change.affected_item_id)
        if item:
            item.status = RoadmapItemStatus.CANCELLED

    elif change.change_type == "promote_medium_to_high":
        item = item_by_id.get(change.affected_item_id)
        if item:
            item.confidence = Confidence.HIGH

    # Other change types modify items or insert new ones via separate mechanisms.
At year end, compare the original January roadmap with what was actually delivered. This retrospective improves the quality of future roadmaps more than any planning framework change:
: what % of HIGH-confidence items in each theme were completed as planned? Below 80 % signals a theme is systematically over-committed.
: of items that started the year as MEDIUM (Q2 plan), what % were actually delivered in Q2? High slippage into Q3+ means quarterly planning is optimistic.
: what % of person-weeks in the year went to items that were not on the January roadmap? High surprise fraction (> 30 %) means the intake process is not capturing real demand.
: did all themes deliver measurable OKR progress? A theme with no completed items after two quarters should be examined for viability.
Too much precision for distant quarters
: detailing Q4 work in January consumes planning time that would be better spent on Q1 execution. Use T-shirt sizes for Q3+.
: a flat list of 40 items with no grouping is unreadable to executives and makes cross-quarter sequencing invisible.
: stakeholders treat a roadmap item as a delivery promise. Set explicit confidence levels and communicate them; MEDIUM is not a commitment.
: sequencing violations are discovered mid-quarter when they block sprints. Run the
check at every quarterly planning session.
No capacity check on roadmap total
: adding items to future quarters without checking aggregate person-weeks creates a plan that is impossible to deliver before it starts.

A multi-quarter GenAI roadmap organises planned work across time and themes, with detail decreasing as distance from the present increases (HIGH → MEDIUM → LOW confidence). Themes are outcome-oriented groupings that map directly to programme OKR objectives. Items are sequenced using explicit dependency types (blocking, enabling, learning), and sequencing violations are caught by automated check before each quarterly plan is committed. Reviews happen monthly (MEDIUM item promotion), quarterly (LOW-to-MEDIUM conversion, capacity check), and annually (theme reassessment). The roadmap is published in two forms: a visual timeline for stakeholder communication and a structured data export for integration with capacity planning and OKR systems.
